g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3. Today we explore two simple hash functions that form the foundation of modern cryptographic hash algorithms.
A hash function converts any length input into a fixed-size string called the hash value or message digest, ensuring data integrity.
Secure hash functions must satisfy three properties: pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, and collision resistance.
g.text: Given m₁, find m₂: same hash

g.data-narration: The Simple Modulo Hash uses basic arithmetic: addition and modulo operation to compress message blocks into a fixed-size digest.
g.text: Core Idea: Process message block by block using addition and modulo operation
Hᵢ = new hash | Hᵢ₋₁ = previous | Mᵢ = block | p = prime

g.data-narration: The algorithm processes the message in four steps: split into blocks, initialize value, iterative processing, and final hash output.
g.text: For each block: add and mod p

g.data-narration: Let's hash 'university' using prime p=13. We'll convert ASCII values: V=86, T=84, U=85. Watch the iterative calculation.
g.text: Message: "university" → ASCII: V(86), T(84), U(85) | IV = 0 | Prime p = 13

g.data-narration: This hash function is cryptographically weak. It's highly prone to collisions and provides almost no diffusion or confusion.
g.text: Weakness: Why This Hash Is Insecure
• Easy collisions (e.g., different messages produce same hash)
• No diffusion/confusion - adjacent blocks barely affect output

g.data-narration: Davies-Meyer uses a block cipher as its core. Message blocks become keys, and the feedforward XOR makes it much stronger.
g.text: Uses a block cipher as compression function. Much stronger construction than simple modulo.

g.data-narration: The algorithm uses message blocks as encryption keys. For each block, encrypt the previous hash and XOR with itself for the new hash.
With a 2-bit cipher, H0=00 and M1=10, encryption yields 11. XOR with 00 gives H1=11. This builds the hash iteratively.
g.text: Assume: 2-bit block cipher | H₀ = 00 (binary) | Message block M₁ = 10

g.data-narration: The feedforward XOR step provides pre-image resistance. Security relies on the underlying cipher strength, making it theoretically stronger.
Comparing both: Simple uses addition and modulo; Davies-Meyer uses block cipher with XOR. One is academically weak, the other conceptually strong.
Remember: both are cryptographically weak and not for real use. They teach foundational concepts leading to secure algorithms like SHA-256.
g.text: Both are EDUCATIONAL ONLY - Not for real-world cryptographic use!

g.data-narration: These simple constructions prepare you for SHA-256 and beyond. They illustrate the evolution from weak academic examples to secure standards.
g.text: Understanding these simple designs prepares you for secure hash functions like SHA-256
Used in SSL/TLS, Digital Signatures, Bitcoin, and more

g.data-narration: End of Module 3. Review the formulas and examples to prepare for your examinations on cryptographic hash functions.
g.text: Module 3 | university 2022 Scheme - CSE | Cryptography & Network Security
